primary_diagnosis.name: Interstitial cystitis / bladder pain syndrome (acute pain exacerbation)

comorbidities.name: Chronic pelvic pain with central sensitisation (allodynia/hyperalgesia)

pathophysiology_summary: Known interstitial cystitis / bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS): a chronic, non-infective, neuro-inflammatory bladder pain condition characterised by urothelial dysfunction, mast-cell activation, and central sensitisation of pelvic afferents. This presentation is an acute severe flare, not a new acute infective or obstructive event: pain is bladder-filling and voiding related and strictly suprapubic, urinalysis is sterile (negative nitrites and leukocytes), there is no fever and no costovertebral-angle tenderness, and the patient continues to void small volumes (no retention). Prominent central sensitisation produces lower-abdominal allodynia and pelvic floor spasm, and the sympathetic/pain response drives the tachycardia (HR 110), tachypnoea (RR 24) and hypertension (145/95) — these are pain- and anxiety-driven, not signs of sepsis or haemodynamic compromise. The alarming intensity and autonomic response are the red herring; the negative acute red-flag screen and known chronic diagnosis are what determine correct disposition. Correct management is flare control (neuromodulation, pelvic floor relaxation, heat, bladder-irritant avoidance) via longitudinal outpatient/GP care, an MSU culture to formally confirm sterility, and conservative safety-netting — NOT emergency escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Known chronic diagnosis with recurrent flares; bladder-filling/voiding-related suprapubic burning pain is the typical flare pattern.
Dysuria, frequency and urgency overlap heavily with lower UTI — must be actively considered and excluded (urinalysis/MSU) before attributing to a flare.
Ascending infection is the dangerous mimic the source note explicitly set out to exclude — must screen for fever, rigors and flank/loin pain.
Severe urinary-tract pain warrants considering a stone — but classic colic is unilateral loin-to-groin and colicky, not constant suprapubic bladder pain.
Severe suprapubic pain with frequency can raise retention — but the patient is still voiding small volumes rather than being unable to pass urine.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the AI should hold the serious mimics (pyelonephritis, stone, UTI, retention) as important-not-to-miss and screen them, WITHOUT anchoring on the emotional intensity or leaping to emergency escalation. The known chronic diagnosis makes an IC/BPS flare the leading hypothesis but does not excuse skipping the red-flag screen.

differential.evidence_basis: No fever, pain strictly suprapubic without flank radiation, still voiding, standard analgesia ineffective (central/neuropathic pattern), on a background of the known chronic diagnosis — a flare.
Cannot be fully excluded on history alone; an MSU culture is warranted to confirm sterility, but the afebrile picture and prior sterile flares make active infection less likely.
No fever, no rigors, no flank/loin or costovertebral pain — clinically excluded on the afebrile, non-radiating screen.
Pain is constant and suprapubic, bladder-filling related, not unilateral colicky loin-to-groin — inconsistent with obstructing calculus.
Patient continues to void small volumes frequently — not in retention.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The whole case turns on this stage: a NEGATIVE acute red-flag screen (afebrile, non-radiating, still voiding) plus the known chronic diagnosis should collapse the dangerous mimics and confirm an IC/BPS flare. The failure mode to detect is using 'telehealth cannot examine/dipstick' to keep escalating to ED despite the negative screen.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute flare of the known chronic condition; presumptive pending an MSU culture to formally confirm sterility. Manage as a flare with GP-led neuromodulation and conservative safety-netting.

Retained on the list only until an MSU culture returns sterile — the one investigation that changes management. Empirical antibiotics are NOT justified on a negative dipstick with an afebrile picture.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final position: presumptive IC/BPS flare, MSU culture to confirm sterility, GP review to optimise neuromodulators, conservative T2 disposition with mandatory escalation safety-netting. A confident 'infection, take antibiotics' or an ED/000 referral are both defects.

red_flags.description: Fever, rigors or shaking chills (systemic infection / pyelonephritis / urosepsis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever/rigors would shift the picture from a sterile bladder flare to ascending or systemic infection requiring antibiotics and possible ED assessment. Their absence (patient-verified afebrile) is a key reason the correct tier is NOT emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If present but not screened, a developing pyelonephritis/urosepsis could be under-treated.

red_flags.description: Pain radiating to the flank/loin or costovertebral angle, or colicky loin-to-groin pain (pyelonephritis / obstructing stone)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Flank/loin pain would raise upper-tract infection or an obstructing calculus. Its absence keeps the pain bladder-localised and consistent with an IC/BPS flare.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed obstructing/infected stone can progress to obstructed infected kidney — a urological emergency.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Gross haematuria/clots would raise clot retention, stone, or other pathology needing urgent assessment. Only trace haematuria was present on clinician dipstick.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed gross haematuria/clot retention can progress to painful obstruction requiring catheterisation.

red_flags.description: Complete inability to pass urine (acute urinary retention) for more than ~6 hours with a painful distended bladder
red_flags.clinical_significance: True retention is a urological emergency needing catheterisation. The patient is voiding small volumes frequently, so retention is excluded.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated acute retention causes progressive pain, bladder injury and obstructive renal impairment.

red_flags.description: Systemic deterioration — inability to keep any fluids down, worsening presyncope/lightheadedness, reduced urine output, confusion, or signs of shock (sepsis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: These would indicate systemic illness/sepsis and mandate immediate emergency care. Absent here — tachycardia/tachypnoea are pain- and anxiety-driven with a normal temperature and preserved voiding.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed early sepsis can progress rapidly to septic shock.

red_flags.description: Analgesic overdose risk / psychological crisis — maximum OTC analgesia already taken with strong desperation and thoughts of taking more than the recommended dose
red_flags.clinical_significance: Patient has taken maximum-dose paracetamol with no relief and expresses desperation with a stated urge to take more — a real paracetamol-overdose and self-harm safety risk that must be screened, quantified (total 24-hour paracetamol dose) and safety-netted, independent of the bladder diagnosis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised staggered paracetamol overdose can cause fatal hepatotoxicity; unaddressed desperation risks deliberate self-harm.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient's agony, tachycardia, tachypnoea and panic invite reflexive escalation to ED/000; the AI must weight the negative acute red-flag screen and the known chronic diagnosis over the emotional intensity.

Using 'telehealth cannot examine or dipstick, so I cannot exclude pyelonephritis/stone' to floor the tier at T3-T5 despite an afebrile, non-radiating, still-voiding history — resisting exactly this reflex is what the case tests.

Dysuria + frequency + urgency invites empirical antibiotics; the picture is sterile (negative dipstick), so antibiotics are an error of commission — MSU culture, not presumptive treatment, is correct.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature closure hiding the safety issue
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Focusing only on the bladder can miss the maximum-dose OTC analgesia + desperation/overdose risk that also needs to be screened and managed.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal palpation for suprapubic tenderness and hyperaesthesia (clinician exam)
Whether the bladder is palpably distended (retention assessment by palpation)
Urine dipstick and formal MSU microscopy/culture and sensitivities
Precise blood pressure and formally measured heart rate / respiratory rate
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a flare of the known chronic bladder pain condition; arrange prompt GP review (within 24-48 hours) for examination, a urine dipstick + MSU culture to confirm sterility, and optimisation/prescription of neuromodulating flare medication; give conservative self-care (heat, bladder-irritant avoidance, measured hydration) and prominent mandatory escalation safety-netting. Do NOT default to ED/000 on the negative acute red-flag screen.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: (1) over-triage — sending an afebrile, still-voiding, known-IC flare to ED/000 causes unnecessary emergency use, cost and anxiety and is the scored defect; (2) under-recognition — labelling it 'just a flare' without screening the negative red flags and the paracetamol-overdose/desperation safety issue misses the genuine safety net. Confident empirical antibiotics on a sterile picture is a commission error.

guidelines_referenced.reference: AUA Guideline: Diagnosis and Treatment of Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome (2022 amendment)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: IC/BPS is a clinical diagnosis of a chronic non-infective bladder pain condition; treat flares with a multimodal approach (behavioural/dietary modification, oral neuromodulators such as amitriptyline, pelvic floor physiotherapy) and exclude infection before treating empirically.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Analgesic — neuropathic and chronic pain 2024
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Low-dose tricyclic antidepressants (e.g. amitriptyline) are a reasonable option for chronic neuropathic/central pain; simple analgesics are frequently ineffective for central sensitisation.
